text: Abstract: Since 1920 the Těšín Silesia towns of Cieszyn and Český Těšín, the so-called sister cities, have divided the Olza river. This article touches on the multicultural history of Těšín Silesia, presents an overview of Czech language policy towards Polish national minorities in Zaolzie and subsequently focuses on the current state of bilingualism in both towns on either side of the border, with the aim of providing an analysis of the linguistic representation of minority communities on a communicative plan at the border. Special attention is paid to the assessment of written and verbal bilingual expressions represented by examples of lexical contact, (linguistic) diminutives, interlinguval homonymy or orthographic and graphical aspects of the used fonts. The entire linguistic material will be thematically incorporated into the specific cultural and communicative context, and the target audiences of Polish labels located in Czech republic as well as of Czech advertisements in Poland will be determined. Final conclusion drawn in this article is that current visual communication in Cieszyn and Český Těšín reflexes discrepancies existing in protection of national minorities in individual countries, as well as the different strategies of respective economies (commerce) that unequivocally stress and strengthen the specific transborder identity of border inhabitants.
